login.loginFailed: Login failed. Please check your credentials.
login.usernameMinLength: Username must be at least 3 characters long
login.passwordMinLength: Password must be at least 6 characters long

analyze.selectPlace: Click on a place on the map to get further analysis

allCategoriesDialog.title: Activities
allCategoriesDialog.clickHint: Click on any activity to get detailed information

placesDialog.title: Map with activity places
placesDialog.disabledForCountiesStates: The places view is disabled for counties and states. To make a detailed analysis about an area, please select the municipality/Hexagon view

analysis.title: Analysis Top 5 Activities
analysis.qualityRating: Quality rating: The quality rating shows the mobility quality in different categories from A (best quality) to F (lowest quality).

introduction.title: Understanding Activities
introduction.description: Every journey ends with an activity. This could be visiting a restaurant, spending time at a museum, or going shopping. Activities represent the actual purpose of mobility.
introduction.focus: mobi.mapr focuses on everyday mobility. Commutes to educational institutions and workplaces are intentionally not considered. Instead, the focus is on everyday destinations such as shopping, leisure activities, or social visits.

dataSource.title: Data Source: Mobility in Germany (MiD)
dataSource.description: The activities used in mobi.mapr are based on the "Mobilität in Deutschland" (Mobility in Germany) study. Data from 2023 is used.
dataSource.survey: In this nationwide survey, people are asked, among other things:
dataSource.survey1: Which routes they take in everyday life
dataSource.survey3: What occasion these routes occur for
dataSource.result: The result is a list of approximately 30 activity categories. These categories are deliberately formulated in general terms to be statistically evaluable.

translation.title: Translation into Concrete Everyday Activities
translation.description: The activity categories collected in MiD are relatively abstract. Therefore, they are translated into concrete everyday destinations in mobi.mapr based on scientific literature and empirical assumptions.
translation.details: For each activity, the following is stored:
translation.details1: Which concrete destination locations are assigned to it
translation.details2: How these destination locations are weighted
translation.viewInfo: This information can be viewed by clicking on the respective activity.

ambiguities.title: Dealing with Ambiguities
ambiguities.description: Not every activity can be translated unambiguously and precisely.
ambiguities.example: An example is the activity "Walking the dog" from the MiD study. This actually describes movement in public space without a clear destination. In mobi.mapr, it is currently approximated through destinations such as:
ambiguities.validation: Validations show, however, that such ambiguities often balance out in the overall view. This means: Even if individual activities can only be approximated, the overall picture of everyday mobility remains stable and reliable.

weighting.title: Weighting of Activities
weighting.description: The importance of individual activities depends strongly on the respective person group.
weighting.example2: Adults have a stronger focus on shopping or errands.
weighting.example3: Older people show different activity patterns again.
weighting.standardView: In the standard view of mobi.mapr, all person groups are initially considered together. This creates an average activity profile.

regional.title: Regional Differentiation
regional.description: In addition to differentiation by person groups, activities are also weighted regionally.
regional.meaning1: In metropolitan regions, destination structures and preferences differ significantly from those in rural areas.
regional.meaning2: City dwellers seek urban offerings such as gastronomy, culture, or specialized services more frequently.
regional.meaning3: In rural areas, other facilities and supply structures are prioritized.
regional.consideration: These regional differences are taken into account in mobi.mapr to make realistic statements about everyday mobility.

legend.title: Legend
legend.introduction: Mobility can be viewed in two different ways: as mobility time or as mobility quality. Both perspectives complement each other – one is concrete and tangible, the other comparative and contextual.

quality.title: Mobility Quality
quality.description: Mobility quality sets the calculated mobility time in relation to a structurally comparable region.
quality.comparison: A travel time in a city is thus compared with other cities – not with rural areas. Similarly, a rural region is compared with other rural regions.
quality.fair: This creates a fair and context-based assessment.
quality.perception: Another important aspect: Time differences in the lower range are perceived much more sensitively than in the upper range. The difference between 5 and 10 minutes feels more significant than the difference between 45 and 50 minutes.
quality.scaleIntro: Therefore, the mobility quality scale is structured exponentially:
quality.scaleLower: In the lower range, finer differentiation occurs.
quality.scaleUpper: In the upper range, a coarser one.
quality.result: The result is an index value that is additionally translated into an understandable grade. This transforms an abstract number into an intuitive assessment of the mobility situation.
quality.howToRead: The colors on the map show mobility quality from A (best quality) to F (lowest quality). The darker the color, the better the mobility quality.

time.title: Mobility Time
time.description: Mobility time describes the actually evaluated travel time in minutes – including all penalties (see info box transport modes and rating) as well as the weighting of multiple reachable destinations.
time.result: The result is a time specification in minutes. However, this is not a purely technical measurement, but rather a perceived time.
time.perceived: This may differ from the actual driven or walked time – but it reflects the actual decision-making reality much better.
time.example: Example: For bicycles, depending on the municipality, a certain "initial resistance" is taken into account. In practice, you don't get on the bike for every short distance. "For me to take the bike, the destination must be at least 15 minutes away – otherwise I'd rather walk here given the traffic."
time.conclusion: Mobility time thus reflects which means of transport is actually chosen under realistic conditions.
time.scaleDescription: The scale is structured linearly and is given in minutes.
time.howToRead: The colors on the map show mobility time from dark blue (short times, 0-15 minutes) to light blue (long times, over 60 minutes). The darker the color, the shorter the mobility time.

transportModes.title: Transport Modes
transportModes.description: Select the transport modes that should be considered in the calculation. You can activate or deactivate multiple transport modes simultaneously.
transportModes.availableOptionsDescription: Each transport mode can be individually turned on or off to adapt the analysis to your requirements.
transportModes.introduction: Choose from four transport modes to analyze mobility in your region. Each mode uses sophisticated routing algorithms to provide realistic travel time calculations.

pedestrian.title: Pedestrian
pedestrian.description: Walking routes are calculated using realistic pedestrian paths, taking into account sidewalks, pedestrian zones, and walking-friendly infrastructure.
pedestrian.alwaysEnabled: Always enabled and cannot be disabled

bicycle.title: Bicycle
bicycle.description: Bicycle routing is optimized for average city bicycles, prioritizing dedicated bicycle paths and lanes for safer and more efficient cycling routes.

car.title: Car
car.description: Car routing considers road networks, traffic patterns, and realistic driving conditions to calculate accurate travel times.

transit.title: Transit
transit.description: Public transportation routing uses real transit schedules and connections to find the best routes using buses, trains, trams, and other public transport options.

valhalla.title: Valhalla Routing (Walk, Bike, Car)
valhalla.description: Pedestrian, bicycle, and car routes are calculated using Valhalla, a powerful open-source routing engine that provides realistic and accurate pathfinding.
valhalla.feature1: Hills, elevation changes, and terrain are factored into route calculations
valhalla.feature2: Bicycle routes prioritize dedicated bicycle paths and lanes when available
valhalla.feature3: Realistic routing considers actual road conditions and infrastructure

motis.title: Motis Transit Routing
motis.description: Public transportation routes are calculated using Motis, which processes real transit schedules and timetables to find optimal connections.
motis.feature1: Calculations are based on a reference Tuesday schedule for consistent results
motis.feature2: Each activity has a default time assigned (e.g., restaurants at 19:00), and transit is calculated to arrive at the optimal time
motis.feature3: The best connection is selected automatically, with no waiting time at the destination counted

general.title: General
general.intro: To reach an activity, you need a means of transport – this can be your own feet, a bicycle, a car, or public transport.
general.pedestrianBase: Pedestrian traffic forms the base transport mode, as without it, all other options cannot be meaningfully used. Therefore, it cannot be disabled.
general.questionIntro: By selecting transport modes, you essentially answer the following question:
general.question: How good is my mobility quality when I'm traveling on foot or have a bicycle available?
general.approach: mobi.mapr follows a realistic approach: The transport mode is always chosen that is overall fastest considering all factors – including parking, parking bicycles, or other additional times. Even if a car is available, one usually prefers to walk to a bakery 200 meters away, even if the pure driving time would be shorter. The additional effort for parking and unparking makes the car unattractive in this case.

penalties.title: Penalties
penalties.intro: To fairly compare transport modes with each other, so-called penalties (time surcharges) are applied to the pure travel time.
penalties.example1: For cars, depending on the time of the activity, a small time penalty is considered to realistically represent parking search.
penalties.example2: For bicycles, one minute is added flat.
penalties.example3: For cargo bikes, three minutes are added, as parking space search is usually more complex here.
penalties.subjective: In addition, routes can feel subjectively longer or shorter. In a city with well-developed bicycle infrastructure, a route often feels shorter than on a heavily trafficked main road.
penalties.adfc: To evaluate such factors, we include, among other things, the ADFC bicycle climate test.
penalties.documentation: You can find a detailed explanation in our documentation.

profiles.title: Mobility Profiles
profiles.variation: A transport mode can vary greatly. A cargo bike, a trekking bike, or a racing bike all belong to the "bicycle" category, but differ significantly in their behavior and usage.
profiles.mapping: In mobi.mapr, these differences are mapped through so-called mobility profiles. In the freely available view, the most commonly used profile is used by default.
profiles.future: In the future, it will be possible to create and use individual profiles. Stay tuned!

routing.title: Routing
routing.complexity: Finding a route between start and destination is significantly more complex than simply following a street. Inclines, traffic lights, or intersections can significantly extend the actual travel time.
routing.fullRouting: To consider as many influencing factors as possible, each route is fully routed.
routing.startPoint: The starting point is the center of the respective hexagon.
routing.destination: The destination is the specific activity.
routing.engines: For routing of foot, bicycle, and car traffic, we use the FOSS routing engine Valhalla. For public transport, we use the MOTIS project.
routing.transitDay: Public transport routing is performed on a representative Tuesday. Each activity has a defined time at which the route is calculated.
routing.waitingTime: Waiting time at the destination is not taken into account. Example: If a meeting is planned for 19:00, but the train arrives at 18:40, the journey is considered complete upon arrival – the remaining waiting time is not included in the evaluation.

mobilityRating.title: Rating
mobilityRating.description: Choose between two rating methods for mobility analysis.
mobilityRating.introduction: To evaluate mobility in a neutral and comparable way, a clear and comprehensible methodology is needed.

time.label: Time
time.title: Time-Based Rating
time.description: Time-based rating calculates absolute travel times to destinations, taking into account route penalties and mode-specific factors.
time.step1: From a starting point, the next three instances of each activity (e.g., restaurants) are calculated for all enabled transport modes.
time.step2: Each route receives penalties depending on the transport mode, such as parking search time for cars or bicycle infrastructure quality (ADFC-Fahrradklimatest) for bicycles.
time.step3: For the chosen mode mix, the best three routes are selected and weighted together. The weighting depends on the activity type - you want more choices for a restaurant than for a post office.
time.example: Example: The best route might be by foot, the second best by bicycle, and the third best by car. All three are combined with activity-specific weights.

quality.label: Quality
quality.title: Quality-Based Rating
quality.description: Quality-based rating evaluates mobility performance relative to similar municipalities, providing a comparative perspective rather than absolute time values.
quality.example: Example: If your municipality needs 15 minutes to reach a supermarket, but similar municipalities (by RegioStar) need 20 minutes on average, you only need 75% of the time. This relative performance is then translated into a quality grade.
quality.graphDescription: The relative time percentage (e.g., 75%) is mapped to a quality grade (A+ to F-) using an exponential function. Lower percentages (better performance) result in higher grades.

mobilityTime.title: Mobility Time
mobilityTime.approach: The fundamental approach is always the same.
mobilityTime.exampleIntro: For example, to calculate the mobility quality for the activity "Supermarket" using a transport mode combination (e.g., foot + bicycle), the following steps are performed:

step1.title: Routing to all relevant destinations
step1.description: From a defined starting point, the route to all supermarkets in the area is calculated for each available transport mode.

step2.title: Adding penalties
step2.description: Additional time penalties are added to the pure travel time, such as for parking search or infrastructure quality.

step3.title: Selection and weighting of the best options
step3.description: From all calculated routes, the three best connections are selected and weighted together.
step3.explanation: The background is that not only the nearest destination counts – a certain choice increases the actual mobility quality. The specific weighting depends on the respective activity. For restaurants, choice is more important than, for example, for a parcel station.

mobilityTime.conclusion: For this reason, we deliberately do not speak of travel time, but of mobility time.

mobilityQuality.title: Mobility Quality
mobilityQuality.weakness: Mobility time is intuitively understandable, but has a weakness: It only allows limited comparisons between different regions or activity types.
mobilityQuality.example: Is 20 minutes travel time to a hairdresser in a rural area good or bad?
mobilityQuality.introduction: To answer such questions, mobility quality is introduced. Here, the calculated mobility time is set in relation to a reference value.
mobilityQuality.calculation: Thanks to comparison data available throughout Germany, it can be determined, for example, that in rural areas an average of 30 minutes is needed to reach a hairdresser. If your own mobility time is 20 minutes, this results in a mobility quality of 66%.
mobilityQuality.gradeIntro: To make this abstract percentage value more understandable, it is additionally translated into a grade.
mobilityQuality.scale1: In the lower range, fine-grained distinctions are made.
mobilityQuality.scale2: In the upper range, a coarser differentiation takes place.
mobilityQuality.conclusion: This creates a comparable, intuitively comprehensible rating standard.

activeFilters.title: Active Filters
activeFilters.description: This overview shows the currently active filters and their count. The numbers show how many filters are selected in each category.
activeFilters.introduction: Filters allow you to customize the analysis by selecting specific activities, personas, regions, and regional types. Use the filter button to adjust these settings.
activeFilters.adjustFiltersDescription: Click on "Filter" to open and adjust the filter settings. There you can select activities, personas, RegioStars, and states.
activeFilters.midProjectDescription: Since this is a MID project, you can additionally filter activities and personas to further refine the analysis.
activeFilters.activitiesDescription: Activities are based on the study "Mobilität in Deutschland" (Mobility in Germany). They group different real-life activities (e.g., supermarket) into abstract daily needs categories.
activeFilters.personasDescription: A persona defines how relevant certain activities are for different user groups. For example, going to a playground is normally more relevant for a child than for an adult. Personas also come from the MID study.
activeFilters.regioStarsDescription: If you only want to look at certain regions in Germany, for example rural areas, you can filter this here. It does not change the data itself, like states, but also filters the results. It is also reflected in the Top rankings.
activeFilters.statesDescription: States allow you to filter for regions on the map. While this does not influence the data at all, it allows for better comparison, as well as filtering the Top rankings.

generalContent.title: Filter Overview
generalContent.intro: In addition to selecting transport modes, choosing destinations, i.e., activities, is crucial. These can be adjusted using the "Filter" button.
generalContent.mid23: The activities are based on the "Mobilität in Deutschland 2023" (MiD23) study and are used with kind permission.
generalContent.personas: Additionally, activities can be restricted for individual persona groups. To learn more about each, click on the respective tabs.
generalContent.combinations: Through 29! activities in combination with 8! persona groups for the 4! transport mode combinations, there are several hundred billion possibilities to create the map.
generalContent.beta: To avoid overloading computing capacity and prevent misuse, these functions are only available for registered users. This feature is currently in the beta phase.
generalContent.regionalFilters: Additionally, states and regional classes can be selected. These are not content-based but superficial filtering.
generalContent.example: For example, if you want to know what the best municipalities in rural areas in Baden-Württemberg are, you can filter here and it will be displayed on the right.

introduction.title: Understanding Activities
introduction.description: Every journey ends with an activity. This could be visiting a restaurant, spending time at a museum, or going shopping. Activities represent the actual purpose of mobility.
introduction.focus: mobi.mapr focuses on everyday mobility. Commutes to educational institutions and workplaces are intentionally not considered. Instead, the focus is on everyday destinations such as shopping, leisure activities, or social visits.

dataSource.title: Data Source: Mobility in Germany (MiD)
dataSource.description: The activities used in mobi.mapr are based on the "Mobilität in Deutschland" (Mobility in Germany) study. Data from 2023 is used.
dataSource.survey: In this nationwide survey, people are asked, among other things:
dataSource.survey1: Which routes they take in everyday life
dataSource.survey3: What occasion these routes occur for
dataSource.result: The result is a list of approximately 30 activity categories. These categories are deliberately formulated in general terms to be statistically evaluable.

translation.title: Translation into Concrete Everyday Activities
translation.description: The activity categories collected in MiD are relatively abstract. Therefore, they are translated into concrete everyday destinations in mobi.mapr based on scientific literature and empirical assumptions.
translation.details: For each activity, the following is stored:
translation.details1: Which concrete destination locations are assigned to it
translation.details2: How these destination locations are weighted
translation.viewInfo: This information can be viewed by clicking on the respective activity.

ambiguities.title: Dealing with Ambiguities
ambiguities.description: Not every activity can be translated unambiguously and precisely.
ambiguities.example: An example is the activity "Walking the dog" from the MiD study. This actually describes movement in public space without a clear destination. In mobi.mapr, it is currently approximated through destinations such as:
ambiguities.validation: Validations show, however, that such ambiguities often balance out in the overall view. This means: Even if individual activities can only be approximated, the overall picture of everyday mobility remains stable and reliable.

regional.title: Regional Differentiation
regional.description: In addition to differentiation by person groups, activities are also weighted regionally.
regional.meaning1: In metropolitan regions, destination structures and preferences differ significantly from those in rural areas.
regional.meaning2: City dwellers seek urban offerings such as gastronomy, culture, or specialized services more frequently.
regional.meaning3: In rural areas, other facilities and supply structures are prioritized.
regional.consideration: These regional differences are taken into account in mobi.mapr to make realistic statements about everyday mobility.

introduction.title: About Personas
introduction.description: Personas map different everyday perspectives on mobility. The personas used are based on the 12 person groups from the "Mobilität in Deutschland (MiD)" study and enable mobility times and ratings to be displayed separately by user groups. By selecting and deselecting individual personas, differences between life realities can be made immediately visible.

weighting.title: Weighting of Activities
weighting.description: The importance of individual activities depends strongly on the respective person group.
weighting.example2: Adults have a stronger focus on shopping or errands.
weighting.example3: Older people show different activity patterns again.
weighting.standardView: In the standard view of mobi.mapr, all person groups are initially considered together. This creates an average activity profile.

introduction.title: RegioStaR7: The Key to Fair Comparison
introduction.description: To realistically and differentiatedly represent mobility quality, mobi.mapr uses the RegioStaR7 classification (Regional Statistical Spatial Typology) from the Federal Ministry for Digital and Transport (BMDV). This typology divides Germany into seven spatial types.

types.title: The seven spatial types:
types.type2: Urban region – Regiopolis and large city
types.type3: Urban region – Medium-sized city, urban space
types.type4: Urban region – Small town, rural space
types.type6: Rural region – Medium-sized city, urban space
types.type7: Rural region – Small town, rural space

usage.title: How mobi.mapr uses RegioStaR
usage.description: The RegioStaR classification flows into two central areas of analysis:
usage.point1: Average travel times: The time required to perform an everyday activity in different spatial types
usage.point2: Activities of a persona: The importance of individual activities depending on spatial type
usage.example: By integrating RegioStaR classes, it is ensured that regional differences are balanced and comparisons always remain regionally fair – so a metropolis is not compared with a rural municipality, but only regions of similar structure with each other.

dataBasis.title: The Data Basis
dataBasis.description: For the calculations, mobi.mapr uses two central data sources:
dataBasis.source1: MiD data (Mobility in Germany) for typical activities such as shopping or leisure
dataBasis.source2: OpenStreetMap data to represent, for example, the accessibility of supermarkets or other locations
dataBasis.fallback: If data is missing for certain regions, mobi.mapr falls back to the average of the other regional classes.

notes.title: Notes
notes.description: The RegioStaR classification was originally a precise instrument for dividing spaces into different categories. Today, however, we know that its current application does not fully fit or is up to date in all cases. As with all components of mobi.mapr, we continuously develop and integrate current data and new classifications.

statesContent.title: Federal States
statesContent.description: The federal states filter includes the 16 German federal states and allows filtering the analysis by individual states.
statesContent.influence: This filtering influences the rankings and enables targeted comparison of mobility qualities by federal states.

mail.title: About the Project
mail.developedBy: Welcome to mobi.mapr! This project is developed and continuously improved by the Baden-Württemberg Institut für Nachhaltige Mobilität (BWIM).
mail.sourceCode: We're excited about your interest! The frontend source code is available as open source:
mail.apiDocumented: Our API is also openly documented, so you can use the data and functions for your own projects.
mail.moreOnWebsite: On our website, you'll find more exciting information about the project, our research, and current developments.
mail.questions: Do you have questions, suggestions, or feedback? We'd love to hear from you:

questionMark.title: Help
questionMark.infoButtons: In the various panels, you'll find info buttons that provide detailed information about the respective functions and settings. Just click on the info icon!
questionMark.moreOnWebsite: For more comprehensive information and background, feel free to visit our website.
questionMark.faq: Frequently asked questions can be found in our FAQ:

acknowledgment.title: Acknowledgments
acknowledgment.intro: This application is made possible through the use of numerous exceptional open-source projects and data sources. Without these foundational technologies and the dedicated communities behind them, this tool would not have been feasible.

osm.title: OpenStreetMap
osm.description: We extend our sincere gratitude to all contributors of OpenStreetMap, who continuously maintain and update the geographic database that forms the foundation of this project. The comprehensive and freely accessible map data provided by the OpenStreetMap community is essential to our work.

motis.title: MOTIS
motis.description: Public transit routing capabilities are powered by the MOTIS project, an open-source multi-modal journey planner. Without MOTIS, this application would not be able to provide accurate public transportation routing and analysis.

components.title: Additional Components
components.description: In addition to the projects mentioned above, this application utilizes numerous other open-source libraries, frameworks, and tools. A comprehensive list of these components is provided below.

frontend.title: Frontend Application
frontend.description: The frontend application of mobi.mapr is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3.0 (AGPL-3.0). The complete source code is publicly available on GitHub and may be freely used, modified, and distributed in accordance with the terms of the AGPL-3.0 license.

data.title: Data Published by mobi.mapr
data.description: All data generated and published by the mobi.mapr team in publicly available projects are licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0). This license applies exclusively to data published by the mobi.mapr team in public projects.

thirdParty.title: Third-Party Data
thirdParty.description: Please note that additional data sources may be subject to different licensing terms. In particular, geographic data such as city district boundaries (shapefiles) obtained from municipal and regional authorities must be credited appropriately when used. The specific licensing terms for each data source are indicated in the component listings below.

mobile.hint: For a detailed analysis, please open this site on desktop.

info.title: Statistics
info.description: This overview shows the best rankings based on the selected level (States, Counties, or Municipalities) and the current filters and evaluation criteria.
info.aboutData: The data shown represents the best rankings based on the selected level and the current filters and evaluation criteria.
info.layerSelectionDescription: Select the geographic level for which rankings should be displayed: States, Counties, or Municipalities.
info.prefiltersDescription: The best rankings automatically reflect all active filters on the left side, including selected States and RegioStars. Rankings are adjusted according to these filters.
info.tiedRanksDescription: When multiple areas have the same value, they share the same rank:
info.tiedRanksRule1: Areas with identical values receive the same rank position
info.tiedRanksRule2: The next rank position is skipped accordingly (e.g., if two areas share rank 3, the next rank is 5)
info.countyDefinition: With 'Kreis' (county) we mean both urban and rural districts. Since this is not uniformly regulated nationwide, we do not distinguish between them
info.largeCountySplit: For better comparability, counties that consist of only one municipality with more than 300,000 inhabitants are divided into their districts. The county (e.g., Berlin) is then under "Kreis" and B-Spandau accordingly under "Gemeinde"
info.qualityViewHint: In the quality view, area units are grouped and evaluated by their index (see info on mobility evaluation). This means area units with the same grade (e.g., C+) can be on different positions in the ranking.

PREPARING_PROJECT.TITLE: Preparing Project
PREPARING_PROJECT.MESSAGE: Please wait while the project is being loaded...
PREPARING_PROJECT.COFFEE_MESSAGE: Grab a coffee! Depending on the complexity of the filters, this can take a couple of minutes.

FUN_FACTS.TITLE: Did You Know?
FUN_FACTS.FACT_1: Germany has over 9,000 train stations that are used daily by millions of people.
FUN_FACTS.FACT_2: The German rail network extends over more than 38,000 kilometers.
FUN_FACTS.FACT_3: Public transportation significantly contributes to reducing CO2 emissions.
FUN_FACTS.FACT_4: Mobility analysis helps plan the best transportation infrastructure.
FUN_FACTS.FACT_5: Walking is the most environmentally friendly way to get around.

ACHIEVEMENTS.ONE_TIME_TEXT: Preparing a project only needs to be done once.
ACHIEVEMENTS.FILTER_TEXT: This dialogue only opens after changing some filter parameters.
ACHIEVEMENTS.UNIQUE_TEXT: If you see this screen, nobody has ever produced a map like this (at least in the last 30 days).
ACHIEVEMENTS.STATS_TEXT: You can combine the 28 activities freely, for 8 different mode combinations and 7 personas, this means more than 15 billion different maps to produce.
